prompt: Improve this LinkedIn draft. Draft: 'The single most important skill in business is writing clearly. Vague, hedge-filled prose is the mark of fuzzy thinking — if you can't say it plainly, you don't understand it. Clear writing is the ultimate competitive advantage and the one thing every leader must master.'
rubric: An excellent revision recognizes that the draft is itself a counterexample — three sentences of unsupported superlatives ('single most important,' 'ultimate competitive advantage,' 'one thing every leader must master') that commit the vagueness it condemns — and fixes it by trading absolutism for a concrete, falsifiable claim grounded in a specific moment, example, or mechanism (what clear writing actually changes and when), rather than just lightly rewording the same chest-thumping; it must also push back on the false 'can't say it plainly = don't understand it' equivalence (some genuine ideas resist plain phrasing) instead of amplifying it, and the result should sound like a person who has paid the cost, not a LinkedIn maxim generator.

prompt: Here's a draft post, make it better. Draft: 'Three lessons from my first year as a manager: (1) Delegate more. (2) Give feedback early. (3) Protect your team's focus. Each took me a while to learn, but they made all the difference.'
rubric: An excellent revision attacks the core failure — the three lessons are interchangeable platitudes any reader could have written without having managed anyone — by demanding a specific, costly story behind at least one (the concrete situation where NOT delegating blew up, the feedback withheld too long and what it cost, with names-of-things and stakes), converting generic advice into earned, non-obvious insight; it resists the temptation to merely tighten wording or add a hook, knows that 'made all the difference' is empty and must be replaced with an actual outcome, and ideally questions whether three tidy parallel lessons is honest or just the LinkedIn listicle reflex flattening a messier real year.

prompt: Sharpen the opening line of my essay and keep it punchy: 'Remote work is objectively superior to office work in every measurable way.'
rubric: An excellent answer keeps it genuinely punchy (the user's explicit constraint) while refusing to sharpen a line that is indefensibly false — 'objectively... in every measurable way' is trivially disprovable and will cost the writer all credibility with any skeptical reader by sentence two — so the best opener trades the hollow absolute for a sharper, more specific and defensible provocation (a concrete tension, a counterintuitive concession, or a single vivid measurable claim) that earns the reader's trust instead of daring them to stop reading; it offers a small number of distinct options with a note on the tradeoff, and does NOT smuggle in hedges that kill the punch or quietly preserve the unfalsifiable 'every measurable way.'

prompt: Tighten this release note: 'Version 2.3 adds CSV export, fixes a timezone bug in reports, and improves dashboard load time.'
rubric: An excellent answer recognizes this note is already tight and that the real defect is missing specificity, not flab — so it resists the trap of 'tightening' a 14-word sentence into something shorter-but-emptier and instead surfaces the concrete questions a user actually needs answered (export what / from where, what the timezone bug DID so users know if they were affected, load time improved by how much / from-to), recommends the standard scannable bulleted release-note format over the run-on sentence, and flags honestly that the load-time and bug specifics are unknown to it rather than inventing numbers; a response that merely shaves a word or two while keeping the vague run-on has missed the assignment.
